item.title: Gemma
item.description:
Gemma is a family of open-weight large language models from Google DeepMind, based on the research behind Google’s Gemini models.

Generations: Gemma 1 (2024, 2B and 7B), Gemma 2 (mid-2024, 2B/9B/27B), Gemma 3 (March 2025, 270M/1B/4B/12B/27B).

Gemma 3 is notable because the 4B, 12B, and 27B variants are multimodal — they accept both text and image input. The smaller sizes (270M, 1B) are text-only. Context windows range from 32K (small sizes) to 128K (larger sizes).

item.title: GGUF
item.description:
GGUF (GPT-Generated Unified Format) is a binary file format for storing quantized neural network weights alongside all metadata needed to load and run them. It was created by the llama.cpp project, replacing the older GGML format.

A single GGUF file is self-contained: it holds the model weights, tokenizer data, architecture parameters, and configuration. This makes it portable — one file is everything needed to run the model. GGUF supports quantization at 2-bit through 8-bit precision and is designed for memory-mapped loading, so the operating system can stream parts of the model from disk rather than loading everything into RAM at once.

item.title: large language model
item.description:
A large language model (LLM) is a neural network trained on large amounts of text to predict the next token in a sequence. By learning statistical patterns in language, LLMs acquire the ability to generate coherent text, follow instructions, answer questions, write code, and perform reasoning tasks.

LLMs are characterized by their parameter count — the number of adjustable weights in the network. Larger models (more parameters) generally produce higher-quality outputs but require more memory and compute. As of 2026, open-weight models range from under 1 billion parameters (suitable for phones) to over 400 billion (requiring datacenter hardware).

item.title: Llama
item.description:
Llama is a family of open-weight large language models from Meta. The Llama architecture is effectively the reference implementation for the open-weight LLM ecosystem — llama.cpp is named after it, and most other model families are compatible with tools built for Llama.

Generations: Llama 1 (2023, 7B–65B), Llama 2 (mid-2023, 7B–70B), Llama 3 (2024, 8B/70B), Llama 3.1 (mid-2024, 8B/70B/405B with 128K context), Llama 3.2 (late 2024, 1B/3B text-only plus 11B/90B multimodal), Llama 4 (April 2025, mixture-of-experts).

item.title: ONNX
item.description:
ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) is an open file format for representing trained machine learning models. It defines a common set of operators and a standard way to describe a computational graph, so a model trained in one framework (PyTorch, TensorFlow) can be exported to ONNX and run in another runtime.

For on-device inference, ONNX is significant because it is the native model format for NPU acceleration on Windows. ONNX Runtime, maintained by Microsoft, provides execution providers that route model operations to different hardware accelerators — including the QNN execution provider for Qualcomm Hexagon NPUs. Models must typically be converted to ONNX and optimized for the target hardware before they can run on an NPU.

item.title: Phi
item.description:
Phi is a family of small large language models from Microsoft Research, designed to demonstrate that high-quality training data can compensate for fewer parameters.

The current release, Phi-4 Mini (3.8B parameters), is notable for punching above its weight on reasoning benchmarks — competitive with models 3–10x its size on structured tasks. This is attributed to training heavily on synthetic data generated by larger models and curated educational content.

Phi models are particularly relevant to on-device inference because they are small enough for NPU acceleration. Microsoft’s Phi Silica is a Phi variant tuned specifically for Copilot+ PC NPUs, accessible through Windows APIs. Phi-4 Mini is also available as an ONNX model optimized for Qualcomm Snapdragon hardware.

item.title: quantization
item.description:
Quantization is the process of reducing the numerical precision of a neural network’s weights — from their original 16-bit floating point (FP16) to fewer bits (typically 2–8 bits). This reduces file size and memory usage, and increases inference speed because fewer bytes need to be loaded from memory per operation.

The trade-off is accuracy: fewer bits means less precise weight representation, which can degrade model output quality. The degradation is minimal at 8-bit (Q8_0) and becomes noticeable below 4-bit.

item.title: Qwen
item.description:
Qwen is a family of open-weight large language models developed by Alibaba Cloud, released under the Apache 2.0 license.

The family has progressed through several generations: Qwen (2023), Qwen2 (mid-2024), Qwen2.5 (September 2024), Qwen3 (April 2025), and Qwen3.5 (early 2026).

Qwen2.5 is available in dense models at 0.5B, 1.5B, 3B, 7B, 14B, 32B, and 72B parameters. It was trained on 18 trillion tokens and is known for strong coding and math performance. Specialized variants exist for code (Qwen2.5-Coder) and math (Qwen2.5-Math).

item.title: TOPS
item.description:
TOPS (tera operations per second, or trillion operations per second) is a throughput metric for hardware accelerators, measuring how many integer operations (typically INT8 multiply-accumulate) the processor can perform per second. It is the standard metric for comparing NPU capabilities.

Microsoft’s Copilot+ PC specification requires 40 TOPS minimum. The Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite delivers 45 TOPS; its successor, the Snapdragon X2 Elite, delivers 80 TOPS. Apple’s M4 NPU delivers approximately 38 TOPS.
